title: Genghis Khan proclaimed at the kurultai
summary: In 1206 a great assembly of Mongol and allied steppe clans gathered at the headwaters of the Onon River and acclaimed a chieftain named Temujin as Chinggis Khaan, ruler of the Great Mongol Nation. It was the formal birth of the Mongol Empire, the state that within a lifetime would become the largest contiguous land empire in history and reshape Eurasia from the Pacific to the Mediterranean.

sections.heading: Background
sections.body:
Temujin was born in the 1160s into a minor clan of the Mongol steppe. His father was poisoned when he was still a child, and he grew up an outlaw and a hostage. Through a mixture of personal charisma, brutal reprisal, and unusually inclusive rewards for followers of any background, he spent two decades knitting the fractious tribes of the eastern steppe into a single confederation.

His campaigns eliminated one rival after another: the Tatars around 1202, the Kereit under Toghrul, and the Naiman at the battle of Chakirmaut in 1204. The Merkits were shattered the same year, and his childhood sworn brother and adult rival Jamukha was executed. By the winter of 1205 to 1206 there was no independent Mongol-speaking power left on the eastern steppe.

sections.heading: What happened
sections.body:
In the spring or summer of 1206 the surviving clan chiefs, generals, and shamans convened a kurultai, the traditional Mongol grand assembly, at the source of the Onon River in what is now northeastern Mongolia. There they proclaimed Temujin ruler of the Yeke Mongghol Ulus, the Great Mongol Nation, under the title Chinggis Khaan, usually rendered in English as Genghis Khan. The exact sense of the title is debated: possible readings include 'fierce' or 'firm' ruler and, via Turkic tängiz meaning ocean, 'universal' or 'oceanic' ruler.

The assembly was more than a coronation. Genghis Khan used it to promulgate a new order. The old tribal lineages were dissolved and every free man aged roughly fifteen to seventy was enrolled in a decimal military structure, in units of ten, one hundred, one thousand, and ten thousand, cutting across kin loyalties. His personal bodyguard, the keshig, was enlarged to ten thousand and made both a household staff and a school for future commanders. A body of customary law, later called the Yassa, regulated theft, adultery, and the treatment of allies, and religious toleration was extended to Christians, Muslims, Buddhists, and Daoists alike.

sections.heading: Aftermath & consequences
sections.body:
The kurultai released a wave of conquest. Between 1209 and 1210 Genghis brought the Tangut Western Xia state to heel. From 1211 to 1215 his armies invaded the Jurchen Jin dynasty of northern China and captured its central capital Zhongdu, the site of modern Beijing. From 1219 he turned west to destroy the Khwarazmian Empire, sacking Bukhara, Samarkand, and Merv in a campaign remembered for its scale of destruction.

By Genghis Khan's death in 1227 his sons and grandsons had inherited an empire that reached from the Sea of Japan to the Caspian. Under his successors it would extend further still, into Russia, Persia, Anatolia, and southern China, forging the Pax Mongolica that briefly made overland travel from Europe to China safer than at any time before the modern age. The 1206 kurultai is the moment historians conventionally date the beginning of that transformation.

worldContext.text: In northern India 1206 was the founding year of another new state. After the assassination of the Ghurid sultan Muhammad of Ghor in March, on the banks of the Jhelum, his trusted Turkic Mamluk commander Qutb al-Din Aibak was recognised as sovereign at Lahore in June, beginning the Mamluk (or 'Slave') line and the Delhi Sultanate that would rule much of the subcontinent for the next three centuries. Qutb al-Din had already begun construction of the great Qutb Minar victory tower at the site of the demolished Rai Pithora fort of the Chauhan Rajputs in Delhi, and of the adjoining Quwwat-ul-Islam mosque incorporating spoliated Jain and Hindu temple columns. He would die from a polo accident in Lahore in 1210 and be succeeded by his son-in-law Iltutmish. Elsewhere in India the Chola dynasty of the Tamil south, still in the reign of Kulottunga III, was declining as Pandya power revived. In the Deccan the Hoysalas ruled from Halebidu and the Kakatiyas from Warangal. In Bengal the Sena dynasty of Lakshmana Sena would soon fall to Bakhtiyar Khilji's raid.

image.caption: The Qutb Minar in Delhi, whose lowest stages were raised by Qutb al-Din Aibak from 1199 and completed by his successor Iltutmish, marking the foundation of the Delhi Sultanate.

worldContext.text: Constantinople was in Latin hands. The Fourth Crusade under the Venetian doge Enrico Dandolo and Boniface of Montferrat had sacked the city on 12 to 15 April 1204, breaking the Byzantine Empire into a Latin Empire of Constantinople, the successor Greek states of Nicaea (under the Laskarid emperors) and Trebizond (under the Grand Komnenoi), and the Despotate of Epirus. In August 1206 Henry of Flanders was crowned the second Latin Emperor in Hagia Sophia after his brother Baldwin I died in Bulgarian captivity following defeat at Adrianople by Tsar Kaloyan. In the west, King John of England, having lost Normandy to Philip II Augustus of France in 1204, landed at La Rochelle in 1206 to defend the remnants of Angevin Aquitaine; his campaign would end inconclusively. Philip's own consolidation of the French royal domain was under way. In the Iberian peninsula the Almohads and the Christian kingdoms were manoeuvring toward the decisive confrontation at Las Navas de Tolosa in 1212. In central Europe the Přemyslid Ottokar I ruled Bohemia, and Pope Innocent III at Rome exercised his high-medieval papal supremacy.

image.caption: The interior of Hagia Sophia in Constantinople, where Henry of Flanders was crowned Latin Emperor in August 1206 after the Fourth Crusade's sack of the city two years earlier.

worldContext.text: The Ayyubid confederation founded by Saladin (Salah al-Din Yusuf ibn Ayyub) after his 1187 victory at Hattin still dominated Egypt, Syria, Yemen, and the Hijaz under the sultan al-Adil (Saphadin, r. 1200 to 1218), Saladin's brother, uneasily managing the reduced crusader states along the Levantine coast after the settlement of the Third Crusade. Cairo, ruled from Saladin's great hilltop citadel, and Damascus, dominated by the Umayyad Mosque, were the two great capitals; the Ayyubids relied heavily on their Mamluk slave-soldier corps. Further west the Almohad caliphate under Muhammad al-Nasir ruled the Maghreb and much of al-Andalus from Marrakesh, though its Iberian position would collapse at Las Navas de Tolosa in 1212 before Alfonso VIII of Castile. In eastern Iran the Khwarazmian empire of Ala al-Din Muhammad II ruled from Samarkand and was in the last years before its catastrophic collision with the Mongols. In the Arabian peninsula the Rasulid dynasty was emerging in Yemen. Sufi orders such as those associated with Ibn Arabi (then in Damascus) and Umar Suhrawardi were flourishing.

image.caption: The Citadel of Saladin above Cairo, begun by Saladin in 1176 and the seat of the Ayyubid and later Mamluk sultans of Egypt.

worldContext.text: China was divided among three states that Mongol armies would soon confront: the Jurchen Jin in the north, ruling from their central capital Zhongdu (modern Beijing) under Emperor Zhangzong (though he died in 1208), the Southern Song in the Yangtze basin and beyond, ruled by Emperor Ningzong from Lin'an (modern Hangzhou) with the powerful chief councillor Han Tuozhou dominating court politics until his assassination in 1207 after the failed Kaixi northern expedition against the Jin, and the Tangut Western Xia in the northwest, whose ruler Li Anquan had usurped the throne in 1206. Song China was still the world's most advanced economy: paper money, gunpowder weapons, magnetic compasses, movable-type printing (invented by Bi Sheng around 1040), coal-fired iron works, and a civil service recruited by written examination all flourished. In Japan the Kamakura shogunate under the Hojo regents (Hojo Yoshitoki succeeded his father in 1205) governed the samurai class in the name of a distant emperor in Kyoto; the monks Honen and, from 1207, Shinran and Eisai were spreading new schools of Pure Land and Zen Buddhism.

image.caption: Along the River During the Qingming Festival, an eighteenth-century Qing court remake of Zhang Zeduan's Song-dynasty original, capturing the bustling urbanism that made Song China the world's richest economy.
image.credit: Chen Mei, Sun Hu, Jin Kun, Dai Hong, Cheng Zhidao (1736 Qing court version), via Wikimedia Commons

worldContext.text: In the West African savanna the empire of Ghana was in terminal decline as new powers rose in its shadow, notably the Sosso kingdom of Sumanguru Kante of the Kaniaga confederation, whose iron-working traditions and reputation as a sorcerer-king would become one of the great themes of the Mande epic. Within a generation Sosso would fall in 1235 at the battle of Kirina to Sundiata Keita and the Malinke founders of the Mali Empire, whose gold-based economy would in the fourteenth century make Mansa Musa the richest man of his day. In the Ethiopian highlands the Zagwe dynasty under King Gebre Mesqel Lalibela (traditionally r. c. 1181 to 1221) was completing the eleven rock-hewn churches at his new capital of Roha, subsequently renamed Lalibela after him; the churches, hewn downward into the volcanic tuff, were intended as a new Jerusalem in response to the Muslim reconquest of the holy city in 1187. In Egypt the Ayyubid state governed the Nile valley. Along the East African coast the Swahili trading cities of Kilwa, Mogadishu, and Mombasa were flourishing on Indian Ocean commerce; Great Zimbabwe on the Zambezi plateau was rising as the centre of a Shona kingdom controlling gold from the Zimbabwean plateau to the coast.

image.caption: The rock-hewn church of Bete Giyorgis (St George) at Lalibela in the Ethiopian highlands, one of the eleven monolithic churches commissioned by the Zagwe king Gebre Mesqel Lalibela.

figures.name: Temujin (Genghis Khan)
figures.role: Mongol chieftain proclaimed universal ruler in 1206; founder of the Mongol Empire, died 1227
figures.credit: Unknown 14th-century Yuan-era painter, via Wikimedia Commons

figures.name: Jamukha
figures.role: Sworn brother and later rival of Temujin, defeated and executed before the 1206 assembly

figures.name: Teb Tengri
figures.role: Shaman who is said to have publicly proclaimed Temujin as khan of the Great Mongol Nation at the kurultai

images.caption: Portrait of Genghis Khan from a 14th-century Yuan-dynasty album, ink and colour on silk. National Palace Museum, Taipei.
images.credit: Unknown 14th-century Yuan-era painter, via Wikimedia Commons
